meta_title: For Freelancers: SaaS pricing to Solve tutorial hell (Beginner Case Study)
meta_description: Learn how to SaaS pricing and overcome tutorial hell. A Beginner-level Case Study using Antigravity and Claude Code.

content.seo_title: For Freelancers: SaaS pricing to Solve tutorial hell
content.seo_description: Learn how to build SaaS pricing models and escape tutorial hell. A Beginner-level Case Study using Antigravity and Claude Code for faster development.
content.answer_block: To escape tutorial hell, stop consuming passive content and start building modular revenue logic. By integrating Antigravity for rapid scaffolding and Claude Code for iterative refinement, you transform abstract pricing theory into a production-ready SaaS feature in under an hour, providing immediate value to your freelance clients.
content.intro:
The screen glows with the 40th 'To-Do list' tutorial of the month. You feel the familiar itch of progress, but deep down, you know the truth: you are stuck in tutorial hell, spinning wheels while your freelance invoice pipeline remains dry. The gap between watching a video and shipping a production-ready SaaS feature feels like a canyon that only experience can bridge.

But what if you stopped building toys and started shipping revenue-driving features? Today, we pivot. We are moving away from passive observation and into the arena by building a dynamic SaaS pricing engine. Using Antigravity and Claude Code, we will bypass the boilerplate and go straight to the architecture that clients actually pay for.

steps.title: Define the Pricing Schema
steps.description: Establish the data structure for your pricing tiers before writing code. This ensures your backend logic remains decoupled from the UI.
steps.antigravity_prompt: Generate a TypeScript interface for a SaaS pricing model including fields for 'name', 'monthly_price', 'yearly_price', 'features' array, and 'is_popular' boolean.
steps.claude_code_command: claude run --prompt 'Create a pricing_schema.ts file with the generated interface and a mock constant array of 3 tiers.'

steps.title: Implement Pricing Logic
steps.description: Create the calculation service to handle currency conversion and discount logic for annual billing cycles.
steps.antigravity_prompt: Create a pricing utility function that calculates the annual savings percentage and returns the discounted price based on a 20% discount rule.
steps.claude_code_command: claude add pricing_utils.ts --content 'Implement the discount calculator function.'

steps.title: Build the UI Component
steps.description: Develop a responsive pricing card component that maps through your schema to display data dynamically.
steps.antigravity_prompt: Create a React component using Tailwind CSS that renders a pricing card for each tier, highlighting the 'is_popular' card with a border glow.
steps.claude_code_command: claude create PricingCards.tsx --style 'modern, minimal, responsive Tailwind'

steps.title: Integrate Toggle State
steps.description: Add a billing interval toggle to switch between monthly and yearly pricing without re-rendering the entire page state.
steps.antigravity_prompt: Write a React hook to manage the billing interval state (monthly/yearly) and export a toggle component.

steps.title: Final QA and Export
steps.description: Run a final audit on your pricing logic to ensure all edge cases, like zero-cost tiers, are handled gracefully.
steps.antigravity_prompt: Review the pricing logic for edge cases including empty feature arrays and negative price values. Add validation.

pro_tips: Always build your pricing schema as a separate JSON or TS file; clients will want to change prices weekly without touching your code.
Use CSS variables for your pricing card highlights so you can re-theme the entire component for different clients in seconds.
Never hardcode currency symbols; use a locale-aware formatter like Intl.NumberFormat to handle internationalization from day one.
Implement 'Stripe-ready' IDs in your data structure so your frontend can pass the correct Price ID directly to the checkout session.
Add a 'Call to Action' button to your pricing cards that accepts a callback prop, making the component reusable across your entire SaaS portfolio.
content.next_steps: Deploy your pricing component to a private Vercel project, integrate a Stripe Checkout session, and use this as a reusable 'base' in your next freelance proposal to justify a premium rate.
content.conversion_block: Stop watching others build and start owning the architecture. With these tools, you aren't just a coder; you're a product-shipping machine. Ready to build? Join our AI Builder community → Start your first project today.
